topic: reflecting on five years in Dubai — gratitude and saudade

turns.content: I'm in a really reflective mood today. I've been in Dubai for a while now and I just kind of feel like... taking stock of everything? Does that make sense?

That makes complete sense. Pausing to reflect on where you are and how far you've come is a really healthy thing to do. What's bringing on the reflective mood — is it an anniversary, or just something that hit you naturally?

it's kind of an anniversary, yeah. I was looking through old photos on my phone and found pictures from when I first arrived here — ai meu Deus I looked so lost haha. I didn't know anyone, didn't know how anything worked, couldn't figure out the metro system. I remember sitting in my empty apartment eating delivery shawarma for the third night in a row thinking 'what did I do?'

That image of sitting alone in an empty apartment wondering what you've done is something almost every expat relates to — that initial period of loneliness and disorientation before the new place starts to feel like home. How does that version of you compare to who you are now?

oh gente, completely different person! I mean, not different — I'm still ME — but I've grown so much. I built a career here, I have colleagues who became real friends, I know the city, I have my routines, my favorite places. I learned how to navigate a totally different culture. I learned how to deal with the heat haha, which honestly was the hardest part in the beginning. Coming from Brazil I thought I could handle hot weather but Dubai hot is a DIFFERENT kind of hot

There's a big difference between tropical heat with humidity and ocean breezes versus desert heat that feels like standing in front of an oven. The fact that you built a full life — career, friendships, routines, a sense of belonging — in a place so different from where you grew up is a real accomplishment. Not everyone who moves abroad manages that. Do you feel like Dubai is home now, or does home still feel like it's somewhere else?

and THAT is the question, querida. That is the million dollar question. Because... both? Dubai is home in the practical sense — my work is here, my daily life is here, my apartment, my bike, my coffee shop where they know my order. But home in the deep sense, like home in my bones? That's still Brazil. That's still hearing Portuguese on the street. That's the smell of rain on hot pavement back home. That saudade never goes away, you know?

Saudade is such a specific feeling — it's not quite homesickness, not quite nostalgia, it's that deep longing for something you love that's far away. And I think for many people who leave their home country, that duality of belonging in two places at once never fully resolves. It's not a problem to fix — it's just part of who you become as someone who's lived in more than one world. Is the saudade always heavy, or does it sometimes feel more bittersweet than painful?

it used to be heavy. Like really heavy, especially the first year or two. I would cry on the phone with my mom, I would hear a Brazilian song and just lose it. But now it's more... gentle? It's like this soft ache that lives inside me. It flares up sometimes — when I cook Brazilian food, when I hear someone speaking Portuguese at the mall, during Carnival season when everyone back home is celebrating and I'm just here at work. But most days it's manageable. It's just part of me now.

That evolution from sharp grief to a soft ache sounds like a healthy adaptation — not that you've stopped caring, but that you've learned to hold the longing without it overwhelming you. The fact that it still flares during specific moments shows how deeply those cultural connections run. Those triggers aren't weaknesses; they're proof of how rich your roots are.

nossa, that actually made me tear up a little haha. But yes that's exactly it. And you know what, I think the gratitude and the saudade coexist. I am SO grateful for what Dubai has given me — professionally I've grown more than I ever imagined, I've met people from all over the world, I've experienced things I never thought I would. But I'm also grateful for where I come from because it made me who I am

That's a beautiful way to see it — carrying your culture forward as a strength rather than just missing what you left behind. The two feelings enriching each other rather than competing makes a lot of sense. What specifically about your growth here are you most proud of?

honestly? The warmth. The way Brazilians connect with people — I bring that into my work every day and my patients feel it. I've had patients tell me that what they appreciate most isn't even my clinical skills, it's that I make them feel like a person, not a file number. And that's so Brazilian, you know? We hug, we touch, we ask about your family, we CARE loudly. I'm proud that I didn't lose that. Dubai could have made me more corporate, more detached, but it didn't

That's a really meaningful thing to preserve. Physical therapy is inherently relational — your clinical knowledge matters, but the human connection is what makes patients trust you, comply with treatment, and ultimately heal. The fact that your cultural warmth is something patients specifically notice and value means it's not just a personality trait — it's part of your clinical effectiveness. That's worth being proud of.

okay now I'm definitely going to cry haha. But happy tears! I think I needed this today — just to sit with all of it and acknowledge it. The hard parts and the beautiful parts. The loneliness of those first months and the fullness of life now. I wouldn't trade this experience for anything, even on the days when the saudade hits hard

That kind of honest reflection — holding the difficulty and the beauty together — takes real emotional maturity. You've clearly built something meaningful here while staying deeply connected to who you are and where you come from. That's not easy, and it sounds like you've done it with a lot of heart.

okay I'm going to stop before I fully lose it haha. Thank you for letting me just... talk this through. Sometimes you need to say it out loud to really feel it. I'm proud of myself. And I'm proud of still missing home, because it means home was worth missing. Ai meu Deus now I really am crying haha. I'm going to go call my mom. Thank you meu bem!

Go call your mom — she'll love hearing from you, especially when you're feeling this open. You should absolutely be proud. Building a life far from home while keeping your roots alive is one of the hardest things a person can do, and you're doing it beautifully. Enjoy the call, and be kind to yourself today.
